title: Career After Marriage
scenario: Your spouse has been offered a prestigious job that requires long working hours (60+ hours/week). This would significantly increase household income but leave little time for family activities.

choices.text: Fully support their career - financial stability matters most
Discuss a balanced schedule - career is important but so is family time
Suggest they find a less demanding role - family should come first
Expect them to decline - a spouse's place is at home

title: Spouse's Career Ambition
scenario: Your partner wants to pursue higher education (PhD/MBA) abroad for 2 years after marriage. This means living apart or uprooting your life.

choices.text: Move with them and support their dream fully
Support them going, manage long-distance for 2 years
Ask them to find a local program instead
Tell them education abroad is unnecessary after marriage

title: Relocation Opportunity
scenario: You receive an incredible job offer in Dubai that would double your income. Your spouse's entire family lives in your current city in Pakistan.

choices.text: Accept it without hesitation - this is a life-changing opportunity
Discuss it together and try for a trial period of 1 year
Consider it only if family can visit frequently
Decline - staying close to family is more important

title: Settling Down
scenario: After marriage, you need to decide where to live. Your family is in Lahore, your spouse's family is in Karachi. Both families want you nearby.

choices.text: Move to a third city for a fresh start together
Choose whichever city has better career opportunities
Live near the husband's family - it's the cultural norm
Alternate between both cities seasonally

title: The In-Law Question
scenario: Your mother-in-law wants to move into your home permanently. Your spouse agrees, but you feel it will affect your privacy and independence.

choices.text: Welcome her warmly - elders deserve our care and respect
Agree, but set clear boundaries about private space
Suggest she lives in a nearby apartment instead
Firmly decline - your home, your rules

title: Joint Family Dynamics
scenario: You're living in a joint family. Your sister-in-law keeps interfering in your personal decisions - what you cook, how you dress, and how you spend money.

choices.text: Accept it gracefully - this is how joint families work
Talk to your spouse privately and ask them to mediate
Politely but firmly tell her to respect boundaries
Insist on moving out to your own place immediately

title: Starting a Family
scenario: You've been married for 6 months. Your in-laws and parents are pressuring you both to have children immediately, but your spouse wants to wait 3-4 years to focus on career first.

choices.text: Side with family - children are a blessing, why wait?
Compromise - wait 1-2 years, then start trying
Support your spouse - career stability first, then children
You're not sure you want children at all

title: Parenting Style
scenario: You disagree with your spouse about raising children. They want a strict, traditional upbringing with emphasis on religion and discipline. You prefer a more liberal, open-minded approach.

choices.text: Follow the traditional path - it worked for our parents
Blend both - traditional values with modern freedoms
Lean towards the liberal approach with some structure
Let children decide their own path when they grow up

title: Household Responsibilities
scenario: Both you and your spouse work full-time. After a long day, someone needs to cook dinner, clean, and help children with homework. Your spouse expects you to handle all domestic duties because 'that's the wife's job.'

choices.text: Accept it - maintaining the home is indeed the wife's duty
Do most of it, but ask spouse to help on weekends
Split everything 50/50 - both work, both share duties
Hire help and neither of you should do domestic work

title: Decision Making Power
scenario: A major family decision needs to be made - buying a new house. Your spouse makes the decision alone without consulting you, saying 'I'm the head of the family.'

choices.text: Respect their decision - the husband leads the family
Accept it this time, but ask to be included next time
Insist that all major decisions must be made together
This is unacceptable - consider it a serious red flag

title: Money Management
scenario: After marriage, you need to decide how to handle finances. Your spouse earns more than you and suggests keeping separate accounts with each paying different bills.

choices.text: Keep everything separate - each person manages their own money
Have a joint account for shared expenses, keep personal savings separate
Pool everything into one joint account - we're a team
One spouse should manage all finances for the household

title: Financial Support to Family
scenario: Your spouse sends 30% of their monthly salary to their parents without discussing it with you. This is affecting your household budget and savings goals.

choices.text: It's their duty to support parents - don't question it
Appreciate the gesture but ask to discuss the amount together
Set a fixed percentage that both agree on for family support
Your household comes first - limit family transfers strictly

title: Religious Practice
scenario: Your spouse expects you to pray five times daily, observe strict hijab/purdah, and attend religious gatherings every week. Your current practice is more moderate.

choices.text: Embrace it fully - strengthen your faith together
Gradually increase your practice to meet halfway
Maintain your current level - faith is personal
Religion should not be imposed - everyone practices differently

title: Interfaith Social Circle
scenario: Your spouse objects to you having close friends from other religions or attending multicultural events, saying it conflicts with Islamic values.

choices.text: Agree and limit your social circle to same-faith people
Reduce frequency but maintain some diverse friendships
Politely disagree - Islam encourages good relations with all
This is controlling behavior - your friendships are your choice

title: Holiday Visits
scenario: Eid is approaching. Your family expects you to spend the entire Eid with them (3 days). Your spouse's family expects the same. Both families are in different cities.

choices.text: Go to husband's family - wife joins husband's household
Alternate - this Eid with one family, next with the other
Split the days - spend some with each family
Celebrate at your own home and invite both families

title: Wife's Business Idea
scenario: Your wife wants to start her own business. She has a solid plan and savings, but it means she'll be very busy for 1-2 years and might need financial support initially.

choices.text: Fully support her - invest in her business together
Support the idea but ask her to start small alongside homemaking
Suggest she waits until children are older
Women running businesses creates family problems

title: Social Media Boundaries
scenario: Your spouse posts family photos on social media and chats with friends of the opposite gender online. You feel uncomfortable about it.

choices.text: Ask them to stop completely - it's inappropriate
Request they limit opposite-gender chats and check with you first
Trust them - everyone has friends of all genders
You both should have complete freedom online

title: Dowry Expectations
scenario: Your in-laws hint that they expected a larger dowry (jahez) and occasionally make comments about what other families gave. This makes your spouse uncomfortable too.

choices.text: Try to fulfill their expectations gradually to keep peace
Ask your spouse to address it with their family firmly
Both of you jointly tell them dowry culture is harmful
This is a dealbreaker - consider it emotional abuse

title: Child's Education
scenario: You want to send your child to an English-medium international school. Your spouse prefers a local Urdu-medium madrasah for stronger religious grounding.

choices.text: Madrasah education provides the best moral foundation
Find a school that balances both - Islamic values with modern education
International school with supplementary Islamic classes
Best possible academic education regardless of religious content

title: Returning Home
scenario: You've been living abroad for 5 years. Your parents are aging and want you to return to Pakistan permanently. Your spouse loves their life abroad and doesn't want to go back.

choices.text: Return immediately - parents need us more than anything
Plan a return within 1-2 years after settling affairs
Visit frequently but continue living abroad
Your life is abroad now - parents can visit or move with you
